title: Predecessors

tex_statement.html: Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $<$ a total order of $J \cup V$. The set of predecessors of $v$ in $G$ are: \[\Pred^G_<(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC.\] We also put: \[\Pred^G_\le(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.\]

lean.title: Predecessors -- strict form
lean.comments:
--
-- `predLt G r v` is the set of nodes `w` of `G` (i.e. `w Ōłł G`,
-- which by `CDMG.mem_iff` is `w Ōłł G.J Ōł¬ G.V`) that are strictly
-- below `v` under the relation `r`. The LN writes this as
-- `Pred^G_<(v)`.
/-
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` (def 3.9,
first equation):

\begin{defmark}
\begin{Def}[Predecessors]
   Let $G=(J,V,E,L)$ be a CDMG and $<$ a total order of $J \cup V$.
   The set of \emph{predecessors} of $v$ in $G$ are:
   \[\Pred^G_<(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC.\]
   We also put:
   \[\Pred^G_\le(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.\]
\end{Def}
\end{defmark}
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Relation `r : ╬▒ ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Prop`, not a `LinearOrder` / not a
--   subtype.** Mirrors the design of `IsTopologicalOrder` in
--   `TopologicalOrder.lean`: the LN writes `w < v` for bare
--   vertices, and every downstream caller wants the same bare-
--   vertex prose. A `LinearOrder` on `Ōåź(G.J Ōł¬ G.V)` would force
--   callers to inject `w` and `v` into a subtype, breaking the LN's
--   notational idiom (cf. `id-algorithm.tex` line 698: "we have the
--   topological order $v_1 < v_2 < v_3$"). The id-algorithm chapter
--   also re-uses `Pred` against substituted graphs --
--   `\Pred^{G_{\doit(C^\cmpl)}}_<(v)` -- which is trivially `predLt
--   G' r v` for the new graph `G'` when `r` is kept separate from
--   `G`.
--
-- * **`Set ╬▒`, not `Finset ╬▒`.** Downstream uses
--   (`id-algorithm.tex:160-241`, `scms3.tex:300`,
--   `scms4.tex:42-48`, `proof-markov_property.tex:57-181`) all
--   treat predecessors as a *set*: intersected with other subsets,
--   unioned across an index, used to index families of co-domains
--   and random variables. The CDMG layer keeps the vertex type `╬▒`
--   fully polymorphic (see `CDMG.lean`'s design notes);
--   `DecidableEq ╬▒` / `Fintype` are deliberately not assumed.
--   Forcing `Finset` here would either impose `DecidableEq ╬▒`
--   everywhere (chapters 4+ use real-valued vertices) or require a
--   `Fintype (G.J Ōł¬ G.V)` hypothesis the LN does not impose.
--
-- * **No `IsTopologicalOrder G r` precondition.** The LN's "let `<`
--   be a total order of `J Ōł¬ V`" is documentational: the set
--   `{ w Ōłł G | w < v }` is well-defined for any binary relation.
--   Bolting an order hypothesis into the signature would force
--   every caller to thread a proof witness for the same `Set`, and
--   would block using `predLt` in the *statement* of claim_3_2
--   (where the existence of a topological order is the conclusion,
--   not a hypothesis). Callers that need the order request it
--   separately, exactly as the LN does.
--
-- * **`v Ōłł G` (the `Membership` instance from
--   `CDMGNotation.lean`), not `v Ōłł G.J Ōł¬ G.V`.** The LN literally
--   writes `w Ōłł G`. The membership instance unfolds to
--   `w Ōłł G.J Ōł¬ G.V` by `CDMG.mem_iff`, so the two are
--   propositionally equal -- but writing `v Ōłł G` keeps the Lean
--   code literally aligned with the LN.
--
-- * **Name `predLt` / `predLe`, not `Pred` or `Pred_lt`.** `Pred`
--   alone clashes with core Lean / Mathlib (`Nat.pred`,
--   `Order.Pred`, ŌĆ”) and would generate disambiguation noise.
--   camelCase `predLt` / `predLe` mirrors Mathlib's conventions
--   (`Set.image`, `Set.preimage`, `Finset.range`) and is
--   immediately recognisable as the LN's `\Pred^G_<` /
--   `\Pred^G_\le`.

/-- LN's `Pred^G_<(v)` -- the *strict predecessors* of `v` in `G`
under the relation `r`: the set of nodes `w Ōłł G` with `r w v`.
Verbatim from `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` def 3.9. -/
lean.statement:
def predLt (G : CDMG ╬▒) (r : ╬▒ ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Prop) (v : ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  { w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v }

lean.title: Predecessors -- weak form
lean.comments:
--
-- `predLe G r v` extends `predLt G r v` by adding `v` itself
-- unconditionally -- exactly the LN's `\cup \{v\}`. Note that the
-- LN does *not* gate this union on `v Ōłł G`: `v` is added even if it
-- is not a node of `G`. The LN is silent about this boundary case
-- (in context, `v` is always a node), but staying literal is safer
-- than second-guessing.
/-
LN's second equation (same `defmark` block as above):

   \[\Pred^G_\le(v) : = \lC w\in G\,|\, w < v \rC \cup \{v\}.\]
-/
--
-- ## Design choice
--
-- * **Body is `{ w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v } Ōł¬ {v}`, NOT
--   `{ w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ (r w v Ōł© w = v) }`.** The two forms differ when
--   `v Ōłē G`: the former still adds `v`, the latter silently drops
--   it. The LN literally writes `\cup \{v\}`, so we follow the LN.
--   Downstream callers that have `v Ōłł G` in context (which is the
--   common case in `id-algorithm.tex` etc.) get the equivalent set
--   via `mem_predLe`; callers that don't get exactly what the LN
--   gives them.
--
-- * **Re-use the existing `predLt` would be possible**
--   (`predLt G r v Ōł¬ {v}`) and propositionally equivalent. We
--   inline the set comprehension so the definitional body matches
--   the LN equation character-for-character. `mem_predLt` /
--   `mem_predLe` give the same `simp` rewrites either way.

/-- LN's `Pred^G_Ōēż(v)` -- the *weak predecessors* of `v` in `G`
under the relation `r`: the strict predecessors of `v`, together
with `v` itself (unconditionally, even if `v Ōłē G`). Verbatim from
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` def 3.9. -/
lean.statement:
def predLe (G : CDMG ╬▒) (r : ╬▒ ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Prop) (v : ╬▒) : Set ╬▒ :=
  { w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v } Ōł¬ {v}
@[simp] theorem mem_predLt {G : CDMG ╬▒} {r : ╬▒ ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Prop} {v w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł G.predLt r v Ōåö w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v := Iff.rfl
@[simp] theorem mem_predLe {G : CDMG ╬▒} {r : ╬▒ ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Prop} {v w : ╬▒} :
    w Ōłł G.predLe r v Ōåö (w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v) Ōł© w = v := by
  simp only [predLe, Set.mem_union, Set.mem_setOf_eq, Set.mem_singleton_iff]

lean_explanation:
The file introduces two definitions on a `CDMG ╬▒`. `predLt G r v` is the set comprehension `{ w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v }`, the strict predecessors of `v` under an arbitrary binary relation `r : ╬▒ ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Prop`. `predLe G r v` is `{ w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v } Ōł¬ {v}`, the strict predecessors together with `v` itself, mirroring the explicit union with a singleton in the source equation.

Both return `Set ╬▒`, keeping the vertex type fully polymorphic. The membership predicate `w Ōłł G` uses the `Membership` instance from `CDMGNotation.lean`, which by `CDMG.mem_iff` unfolds to `w Ōłł G.J Ōł¬ G.V`; writing `w Ōłł G` keeps the Lean literally aligned with the prose `w \in G`.

Two `@[simp]` lemmas expose the unfoldings. `mem_predLt` is discharged by `Iff.rfl` since the set is definitionally a `setOf`. `mem_predLe` is closed by `simp only [predLe, Set.mem_union, Set.mem_setOf_eq, Set.mem_singleton_iff]`, normalising membership in the union into the disjunction `(w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v) Ōł© w = v`. The camelCase names `predLt` / `predLe` follow Mathlib conventions (`Set.image`, `Finset.range`) and avoid clashing with `Nat.pred` and `Order.Pred`.
design_choices:
The relation argument is a bare `r : ╬▒ ŌåÆ ╬▒ ŌåÆ Prop` rather than a `LinearOrder` on a subtype: this mirrors the design of `IsTopologicalOrder` in `TopologicalOrder.lean` and preserves the lecture-notes idiom of writing `w < v` on bare vertices. A `LinearOrder` on `Ōåź(G.J Ōł¬ G.V)` would force callers to inject vertices into a subtype, and the id-algorithm chapter reuses `Pred` against substituted graphs, which is trivially `predLt G' r v` when `r` is kept separate from `G`.

The return type is `Set ╬▒`, not `Finset ╬▒`, because downstream uses in `id-algorithm.tex`, `scms3.tex`, `scms4.tex`, and `proof-markov_property.tex` treat predecessors as sets to be intersected, unioned, and used as indices; the `CDMG` layer deliberately keeps the vertex type polymorphic without `DecidableEq` or `Fintype`, and chapters 4+ use real-valued vertices.

No `IsTopologicalOrder G r` precondition is imposed: the set is well-defined for any binary relation, and bolting in an order hypothesis would block using `predLt` in the statement of `claim_3_2`, where existence of a topological order is the conclusion.

Membership is written `v Ōłł G` rather than `v Ōłł G.J Ōł¬ G.V` to stay literally aligned with the source. For `predLe`, the body is `{ w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ r w v } Ōł¬ {v}` rather than `{ w | w Ōłł G Ōł¦ (r w v Ōł© w = v) }`; the two diverge when `v Ōłē G`, and the literal union-with-singleton form is preserved. The set comprehension is inlined rather than reusing `predLt G r v Ōł¬ {v}` so the body matches the source equation character-for-character. The names `predLt` / `predLe` avoid clashing with `Nat.pred` / `Order.Pred`.
